docs.title: OpenSling WikiBuild water-cleaning machines that can survive real field work.
docs.text:
OpenSling is a practical wiki for treatment trains, hardware modules, validation workflows, and operating practices that turn rough water into reliably cleaner output.

4 Core tracks: foundations, systems, builds, operations 3 Starter builds: bench prototype, pilot skid, reusable test rig 1 Rule that matters: every claim needs a measurement plan

docs.title: What This Wiki Is For
docs.text:
OpenSling focuses on modular machines for cleaning water, not abstract product marketing. The working assumption is that useful systems are:

  • measurable in the field
  • repairable with ordinary tools
  • safe to operate around electricity, biohazards, and pressure
  • explicit about what contaminants they do and do not address
Treatment logic

Start with source water, contaminant classes, and staged removal instead of jumping straight to a single magic filter.

Module contracts

Define clean interfaces between intake, solids removal, fine filtration, disinfection, sensing, and power.

Buildable hardware

Prefer parts that can be sourced, serviced, and swapped without exotic fabrication.

Validation first

Every prototype should ship with sampling points, instrumentation, and a test plan.

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text:
1. Read Mission and Scope to understand what OpenSling is trying to build.
  2. Use Design Principles before drafting a new machine or subsystem.
  3. Map your water source against Contaminants and Constraints.
  4. Build from the Treatment Train rather than from a single component.
  5. Pick a starting implementation from the Build Guides.

docs.title: Reference Flow
docs.text:
The default machine pattern documented here is:

  1. intake and coarse debris exclusion
  2. bulk solids removal through settling, screening, or cyclone-style separation
  3. fine filtration for suspended load reduction
  4. adsorption or chemistry steps when dissolved contaminants matter
  5. disinfection
  6. clean storage, monitored output, and maintenance logging
Scope note: this wiki is an engineering workspace. It documents methods, tradeoffs, and validation practices, but any system intended for drinking water still needs source-specific testing, local regulatory review, and responsible operation.

docs.title: Current Priorities
docs.text:
• establish a robust bench prototype that is easy to instrument
  • converge on a field pilot skid with standard plumbing and service loops
  • create repeatable sampling and maintenance procedures
  • track open research questions around cost, fouling, and contaminant-specific treatment

docs.title: About OpenSling
docs.text: OpenSling is a working wiki for people building machines that clean water. The project is opinionated in one useful way: hardware ideas are only valuable when they can be documented, instrumented, tested, serviced, and improved in public.

docs.title: What the Project Optimizes For
docs.text:
• modular treatment systems instead of monolithic machines
  • straightforward plumbing, wiring, and maintenance access
  • explicit treatment claims tied to measurement plans
  • build notes that future contributors can reproduce without oral history

docs.title: What Lives Here
docs.text:
This repository holds:

  • design notes for treatment architecture
  • build guides for prototype and pilot hardware
  • operations playbooks for testing, maintenance, and safety
  • research backlogs for unresolved engineering questions

docs.title: What It Does Not Claim
docs.text: OpenSling does not assume that one hardware stack works for every water source. Different contaminant loads require different treatment steps, and any potable-water use case needs appropriate validation and local compliance review.

docs.title: Stack
docs.text:
The wiki keeps the same underlying structure as sloppy.wiki:

  • MkDocs
  • Material for MkDocs
  • GitHub Actions deployment
  • lightweight shell helpers for local setup, serving, and builds

docs.title: Contribution Model
docs.text:
The expected contribution style is simple:

  1. document the problem and source-water assumptions
  2. document the build or operational change
  3. attach measurements, photos, or failure notes
  4. leave the next person with enough context to continue

Project source: github.com/pierce403/opensling

docs.title: Bench Prototype
docs.text: The bench prototype is the fastest way to validate the OpenSling treatment logic before committing to a field-ready chassis.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text:
Build a reconfigurable loop that allows quick changes to:

  • intake geometry
  • solids removal stage
  • cartridge selection
  • pressure instrumentation
  • disinfection method

docs.title: Suggested Starter Stack
docs.text: Stage Example approach Feed open test reservoir with screened pickup Pump 12 VDC diaphragm or centrifugal pump sized for moderate flow Pre-separation small settling chamber or cyclone test section Fine filtration clear-housing sediment cartridge Polishing optional carbon block in bypass or full-flow mode Disinfection UV chamber or placeholder spool for later comparison

docs.title: Build Priorities
docs.text:
1. mount gauges before and after fine filtration
  2. include sample valves at inlet, post-filtration, and final outlet
  3. make drains easy to use without spraying the work area
  4. label flow direction on every removable module

docs.title: Bench Success Criteria
docs.text:
• repeatable flow measurement
  • visible and measurable change across at least one treatment stage
  • easy cartridge or module swap within minutes
  • logging or manual recording procedure defined before the first test

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: This prototype is for process development. If the system is later aimed at potable use, validation requirements become much stricter than "it looked better on the bench."

docs.title: Field Pilot Skid
docs.text: The field pilot skid packages the same treatment logic as the bench rig into a machine that can be transported, serviced, and observed during longer runs.

docs.title: Pilot Goals
docs.text:
• survive transport and setup without replumbing
  • support repeated service cycles in one workday
  • carry enough sensing to explain failures instead of hiding them
  • keep dirty-side and clean-side handling physically separate

docs.title: Layout Pattern
docs.text:
Arrange the skid in treatment order from one side to the other:

  1. intake and feed connections
  2. solids handling and dirty drains
  3. fine filtration and differential pressure instrumentation
  4. polishing or disinfection
  5. clean outlet and storage connection
  6. controls and power above the wet zone

docs.title: Commissioning Checklist
docs.text:
• verify all hoses and unions are tightened
  • flush the machine with non-critical water before first sampling
  • log baseline pressures at clean-filter condition
  • test dry-run and overpressure protections
  • confirm sampling points are accessible with the machine operating

docs.title: Serviceability Rules
docs.text:
• no filter change should require moving the entire skid
  • electrical panels should open without exposing wet plumbing
  • common service parts should be visible at a glance
  • the operator should be able to isolate modules for troubleshooting

docs.title: Test Rig
docs.text: The test rig is separate from the treatment machine itself. Its job is to compare modules and procedures under controlled conditions so changes are based on data instead of memory.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• compare two pumps under the same head and flow conditions
  • measure pressure drop across candidate filter housings
  • test fouling behavior with different pre-separation strategies
  • validate alarms and control thresholds before field deployment

docs.title: Rig Requirements
docs.text:
• stable feed reservoir
  • repeatable plumbing geometry
  • simple isolation valves
  • instrument mounts for temporary sensors
  • a logging sheet or digital logger that captures each run

docs.title: Recommended Run Record
docs.text: Field Example Test ID TR-2026-03-13-01 Source water pond water after coarse screening Module under test 5 micron sediment cartridge Starting pressure drop 1.2 psi Ending pressure drop 4.8 psi Flow rate 2.1 L/min Observation visible fouling by fine clay, no leaks

docs.title: Bias to Avoid
docs.text: Do not compare modules under changing source water without noting it. Test rigs only help when the conditions are captured honestly.

docs.title: Contaminants and Constraints
docs.text: Water-cleaning machines fail when the design ignores what is actually in the water. Start by classifying the source, then choose treatment stages that target the relevant loads.

docs.title: Major Contaminant Classes
docs.text: Class Examples Typical consequence Common responses Gross debris leaves, sand, fibers, trash pump wear, clogs, false sensor readings screens, settling, sump design Suspended solids silt, clay, rust, organic fines turbidity, fouling, shortened filter life settling, hydrocyclones, cartridge or membrane filtration Biological load bacteria, protozoa, algae health risk, slime formation, odor disinfection, fouling control, storage hygiene Dissolved organics tannins, oils, industrial residues taste, odor, membrane stress, uncertain toxicity activated carbon, specialized media, source-specific analysis Dissolved salts and metals hardness, arsenic, lead, nitrate chemistry limits not solved by simple filters source-specific treatment such as ion exchange or membranes

docs.title: Constraints That Change the Machine
docs.text:
The right treatment stack depends on more than contamination alone:

  • available power and duty cycle
  • target flow rate
  • allowable maintenance interval
  • operator skill level
  • local availability of replacement parts
  • whether the output is for wash water, process water, or potential drinking use

docs.title: Source Characterization Checklist
docs.text:
• identify the water source and collection method
  • record visible solids, color, odor, and seasonality
  • estimate expected flow range
  • note likely upstream contamination sources
  • define the use case for treated water
  • decide what must be measured at minimum

Warning

Do not assume "looks clear" means "safe." Visual improvement and microbial safety are separate questions.

docs.title: Energy and Maintenance Tradeoffs
docs.text: Water treatment hardware is usually constrained by maintenance long before it is constrained by theory. Energy and service burden should be considered together.

docs.title: Tradeoff Table
docs.text: Choice Strength Cost Larger pre-separation volume reduces filter fouling adds bulk and slower startup Higher-pressure fine filtration better particulate control more pump power and more complex leak management UV disinfection no chemical residual needs clear water, power, and lamp maintenance Carbon polishing improves taste and odor consumable replacement and uncertain performance on all chemistries Solar-backed system off-grid operation variable duty cycle and battery management overhead

docs.title: Maintenance Reality
docs.text:
When comparing designs, ask:

  • how often does an operator touch the machine?
  • what parts clog first?
  • can the service task be done without contaminating the clean side?
  • how many tools are required?
  • what happens if service is skipped for a week?

docs.title: Useful Biases
docs.text:
• spend volume and plumbing on upstream solids management
  • keep consumables standardized
  • make the dirty side physically obvious
  • expose maintenance intervals as checklists, not tribal knowledge

docs.title: Power Budget Categories
docs.text:
Category Example loads Always on controller, telemetry, low-power sensors Duty cycled pumps, valves, UV chamber Rare events flush solenoids, alarms, work lights

Documenting the power budget alongside the maintenance budget makes it easier to design systems that actually stay deployed.

docs.title: Treatment Train
docs.text: A treatment train is a sequence of modules, each doing one job well enough that the next stage can work reliably. OpenSling assumes staged treatment is easier to debug and maintain than all-in-one boxes.

docs.title: Baseline Treatment Sequence
docs.text:
1. Intake and exclusion: keep large debris and air ingestion out of the pump path.
  2. Bulk solids reduction: remove heavy particulate load early to protect downstream stages.
  3. Fine filtration: reduce suspended solids to a level the final treatment stages can tolerate.
  4. Conditioning or adsorption: use when dissolved contaminants, taste, or odor matter.
  5. Disinfection: reduce biological risk with a method matched to flow, clarity, and power.
  6. Protected storage: prevent recontamination after treatment.

docs.title: Why the Order Matters
docs.text: Disinfection works better when turbidity is already reduced. Carbon lasts longer when solids are already managed. Pumps and sensors behave better when intake debris is controlled. The sequence is not arbitrary.

docs.title: Module Selection Questions
docs.text: Stage Decide this first Typical options Intake How dirty is the source mechanically? basket strainer, screened foot valve, settling inlet Solids removal Is the load mostly heavy grit or fine suspended solids? settling tank, swirl separator, hydrocyclone, bag filter Fine filtration What pressure drop and consumable budget are acceptable? sediment cartridges, membrane modules Adsorption Are organics, chlorine byproducts, or odors a concern? activated carbon block, granular carbon Disinfection What contact time and power budget are available? UV-C, chlorination, ozone in specialized cases

docs.title: Design Hint
docs.text: Place sample points and pressure gauges around the stages that are most likely to foul. A treatment train without observability becomes guesswork fast.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: This wiki should accumulate usable engineering knowledge, not just polished summaries. Contributions are most helpful when they leave a clear trail from idea to implementation to measured result.

docs.title: What to Contribute
docs.text:
• new build guides
  • updates to treatment assumptions
  • field notes from prototype runs
  • maintenance findings and failure reports
  • component comparisons with documented reasoning

docs.title: Minimum Contribution Standard
docs.text:
Every substantive addition should answer:

  • What problem was being solved?
  • What water source or operating context was assumed?
  • What changed in the design or process?
  • What did we measure afterward?
  • What remains uncertain?

docs.title: Suggested Page Pattern
docs.text:
Use a structure like this when adding a new technical page:

# Title

## Purpose

## Context and Assumptions

## Build or Procedure

## Measurements

## Failure Modes

## Next Iteration

docs.title: Git Workflow
docs.text:
1. create or edit pages under docs/
  2. preview locally with ./serve.sh
  3. run ./build.sh before pushing
  4. commit with a message that names the system or process you changed

docs.title: Writing Style
docs.text:
• prefer precise statements over marketing language
  • state units for all measurements
  • separate facts, assumptions, and next-step hypotheses
  • use tables when comparing options
  • keep photos and diagrams tied to the specific hardware revision they show

docs.title: Failure Reports
docs.text:
A good failure report is a contribution, not a setback. Include:

  • what failed
  • when it failed
  • what the machine was doing at the time
  • how the operator recovered
  • what change should be tested next

docs.title: Design Principles
docs.text: These principles are intended to keep the project grounded when design choices start multiplying.

docs.title: Core Rules
docs.text: Principle What it means in practice Modular Each treatment function should be swappable without rebuilding the whole machine. Measurable Every stage needs inlet and outlet observations, not just faith in the component label. Repairable A field operator should be able to open, clean, and replace common wear items quickly. Honest about limits If a module only handles turbidity and sediment, document that clearly. Energy-aware Power budgets matter, especially for remote or solar-backed deployments. Safe by default Pressure, electricity, UV exposure, and biohazard handling need explicit safeguards.

docs.title: Preferred Patterns
docs.text:
• clear plumbing runs over tightly packed assemblies
  • common voltages and connectors over custom one-off wiring
  • sample ports before and after major treatment stages
  • consumables that can be sourced from multiple vendors
  • documentation that states the source-water scenario up front

docs.title: Avoid
docs.text:
• hidden dependencies between modules
  • treatment claims without a validation plan
  • filter stacks that are impossible to inspect or clean
  • electronics mounted where leaks naturally drain
  • ambiguous versioning of mechanical assemblies

docs.title: Definition of Done
docs.text:
A subsystem is ready to share when it has:

  • a purpose statement
  • a schematic or plumbing map
  • a bill of materials or sourcing path
  • operating assumptions
  • failure modes worth watching
  • at least one measurable success criterion

docs.title: Design Review Questions
docs.text:
Before adopting a new concept, answer these directly:

  1. Which contaminant class is this stage meant to reduce?
  2. What measurement shows whether it is working?
  3. What fouls or wears first?
  4. What does service look like after 50 hours, 200 hours, and one bad day in the field?
  5. Can a contributor reproduce the design without asking the original builder?

docs.title: Mission and Scope
docs.text: OpenSling exists to document how to build machines that make dirty water cleaner in a measurable way. The emphasis is on practical systems engineering: treatment stages, component choices, testing plans, and operating procedures.

docs.title: Working Mission
docs.text:
Build an open, modular reference stack for water-cleaning hardware that is:

  • understandable by new contributors
  • instrumented enough to debug
  • repairable without vendor lock-in
  • explicit about source-water assumptions

docs.title: Primary Deliverables
docs.text:
The first version of this wiki is organized around three tangible outputs:

  1. a bench prototype that is easy to modify and measure
  2. a field pilot skid that packages the same logic into serviceable hardware
  3. a reusable test rig for comparing pumps, filters, and treatment stages

docs.title: In Scope
docs.text:
• intake, pumping, plumbing, and packaging decisions
  • solids removal, filtration, adsorption, and disinfection stages
  • sensor selection, control logic, and data logging
  • maintenance procedures and failure analysis
  • documentation standards for experiments and field notes

docs.title: Out of Scope
docs.text:
• claims that a generic design is safe for every water source
  • industrial compliance advice for specific jurisdictions
  • replacing laboratory validation with informal observation

docs.title: How to Use the Wiki
docs.text:
If you are new:

  1. start with Design Principles
  2. read the water-side context in Contaminants and Constraints
  3. choose a system concept in Modular Architecture
  4. build something concrete from the Bench Prototype

Note

OpenSling treats documentation as part of the hardware. A prototype without assumptions, measurements, and failure notes is incomplete.

docs.title: Maintenance and Failure Modes
docs.text: Maintenance is not what happens after the design is finished. The maintenance path is part of the design.

docs.title: Service Cadence Template
docs.text: Interval Typical tasks Before each run inspect hoses, power wiring, valves, and drains Daily flush solids traps, log pressures, inspect leaks Weekly replace or clean sacrificial filters as needed, inspect connectors Monthly review fouling trends, inspect pump wear items, verify sensor calibration

docs.title: Failure Modes Worth Tracking
docs.text:
• intake clogging
  • air ingress on suction side
  • pump cavitation or overheating
  • filter bypass due to bad seals
  • pressure creep from fouled cartridges
  • UV failure, low intensity, or contact-time mismatch
  • cross-contamination during maintenance

docs.title: Good Maintenance Design
docs.text:
• hand-accessible service points
  • drains that do not soak the electronics bay
  • plain labels on consumables and replacement intervals
  • enough physical room to inspect seals before closing the system

docs.title: Failure Report Template
docs.text:
1. what failed
  2. how the issue was detected
  3. what the operating state was at the time
  4. immediate containment or recovery step
  5. design or procedure change proposed next

docs.title: Safety
docs.text: Water treatment hardware mixes electricity, pressure, biological exposure, and sometimes UV or chemicals. Treat safety as design work, not a footnote.

docs.title: Core Hazards
docs.text: Hazard Typical source Control approach Electrical shock wet connectors, damaged insulation, poor grounding sealed connectors, strain relief, isolation, inspections Pressure release clogged filters, dead-headed pumps, trapped air relief paths, gauges, shutdown logic Biological exposure raw water handling, sludge removal, contaminated surfaces gloves, containment, cleaning procedures UV exposure open or damaged UV chamber shielding, interlocks, service lockout Chemical mishandling disinfectants or cleaning agents labeling, PPE, storage discipline

docs.title: Operator Rules
docs.text:
• isolate power before opening electrical enclosures
  • depressurize wet-side modules before service
  • treat sludge and dirty consumables as contaminated waste
  • document any bypass or safety override used during testing

docs.title: Shop and Field Readiness
docs.text:
Before running a machine outside the bench:

  • verify emergency shutdown behavior
  • check that all drains route away from electrical equipment
  • confirm that service tools and PPE are physically nearby
  • make sure the operator can identify clean-side versus dirty-side components quickly

docs.title: Sampling and Validation
docs.text: If the project cannot show what changed between inlet and outlet, it is not engineering yet. Sampling plans are part of the machine design.

docs.title: Minimum Sampling Points
docs.text:
• raw source water
  • after bulk solids removal
  • after fine filtration
  • final outlet or storage point

docs.title: Record for Every Sample
docs.text: Field Why it matters time and date tracks drift over the run machine revision avoids mixing results across hardware changes source description water quality may vary by location and season flow rate treatment performance is often flow-dependent observed turbidity, color, odor fast field context even when lab data comes later operator notes captures anomalies that instruments miss

docs.title: Validation Mindset
docs.text:
Use the simplest metric that answers the current question, then expand as the design matures:

  • turbidity or optical clarity trends for solids handling
  • pressure rise for fouling behavior
  • treated volume before service for maintenance planning
  • source-specific lab tests when the target use case requires them

docs.title: Common Failure
docs.text: Sampling only when the machine is freshly cleaned produces misleading optimism. Include dirty-state and end-of-run measurements.

docs.title: Component Sourcing
docs.text: Component selection should support iteration speed and field maintenance, not just nominal spec-sheet performance.

docs.title: Sourcing Heuristics
docs.text:
• prefer parts with multiple suppliers
  • avoid housings and cartridges that lock the project into one brand
  • record connector types, thread standards, and sealing materials
  • note lead times for items that can block rebuilds

docs.title: Priority Categories
docs.text: Category What to capture Pumps flow range, suction behavior, service kits, voltage Filters and housings micron rating, pressure drop, seal material, replacement cost Sensors interface type, calibration needs, ingress protection Fittings and valves thread standard, body material, service access Power components charging profile, fuse strategy, field replaceability

docs.title: Vendor Notes
docs.text:
Keep sourcing notes practical:

  • what arrived quickly
  • what was packaged poorly
  • what had misleading documentation
  • what worked but was painful to service

The goal is not a shopping catalog. It is a memory system for future build decisions.

docs.title: Open Questions
docs.text: This page tracks engineering questions that should turn into experiments, not endless discussion.

docs.title: Current Backlog
docs.text:
• How much upstream solids reduction is worth the added bulk before cartridge life gains flatten out?
  • Which pump style gives the best balance of suction tolerance, serviceability, and power draw for the pilot skid?
  • What minimal field instrumentation gives enough signal to predict fouling before throughput collapses?
  • Which treatment stages deserve bypass loops for comparative testing?
  • What enclosure layout best separates wet maintenance from controls access?

docs.title: Converting Questions Into Work
docs.text:
For each question, define:

  • the hypothesis
  • the metric
  • the test setup
  • the run length
  • the decision that will be made afterward

docs.title: Good Example
docs.text:
Instead of asking "Is this filter better?", ask:

Does adding a cyclone-style pre-separation stage increase treated volume before a 5 micron cartridge reaches the service threshold?

docs.title: Modular Architecture
docs.text: OpenSling treats a water-cleaning machine as a chain of interfaceable modules. Each module should have a clear role, expected inlet conditions, and a bounded maintenance story.

docs.title: Reference Modules
docs.text: Module Purpose Typical interfaces Intake draw from source while excluding gross debris hose, foot valve, coarse screen Solids module remove heavy particulate load reservoir, cyclone, drain loop Fine filtration module lower suspended solids filter housings, differential pressure ports Polishing module adsorption or chemistry-specific treatment inline canister, bypass manifold Disinfection module reduce biological load UV chamber or chemical dosing zone Controls module coordinate sensors, pumps, alarms, logs low-voltage power, I/O harness Power module supply and protect electrical loads battery, DC bus, charging input

docs.title: Interface Contract
docs.text:
Every module page should eventually define:

  • flow range
  • pressure range
  • electrical needs
  • service interval
  • failure signals
  • acceptable upstream and downstream conditions

docs.title: Packaging Advice
docs.text:
• keep the dirty path and clean path visually distinct
  • mount service items where they can be replaced without pulling other modules
  • place drains and flush points intentionally
  • leave physical room for instrumentation upgrades

docs.title: Versioning
docs.text: Use simple revision labels on assemblies and manifolds. If a field note mentions "pilot skid rev B", the plumbing map should make that traceable.

docs.title: Power and Enclosure
docs.text: The machine enclosure and power system decide whether a design survives transport, weather, and operator abuse. Treat them as first-class engineering problems.

docs.title: Power Architecture
docs.text:
Start with a written list of loads and duty cycles:

  • pumps
  • controls and sensors
  • UV or other disinfection loads
  • valves or actuators
  • communication hardware

docs.title: Practical Defaults
docs.text:
• use a common DC distribution voltage where possible
  • separate noisy motor power from sensitive sensor wiring
  • fuse modules individually
  • label every connector that might be unplugged in the field

docs.title: Enclosure Design Rules
docs.text: Rule Reason Keep electronics above probable leak paths water always finds the lowest cable loop Provide deliberate cable strain relief transport vibration is unforgiving Leave service clearance around filter housings cartridge changes are routine, not edge cases Vent or manage heat intentionally sealed boxes and UV supplies can run hot

docs.title: Environmental Considerations
docs.text:
• sun exposure and enclosure temperature
  • splash, rain, and hose-down events
  • mud and sediment on moving parts
  • transport shock
  • operator access with gloves or wet hands

docs.title: Sensors and Controls
docs.text: Without instrumentation, you cannot tell whether a machine improved water quality or just moved the problem downstream.

docs.title: Baseline Sensor Set
docs.text: Sensor Why it matters Placement Flow verifies throughput and helps normalize performance main treated-water line Pressure shows restriction and fouling before and after filters Tank level protects pumps and supports automation feed and clean tanks Turbidity or optical proxy tracks solids reduction trends inlet and post-filtration Conductivity or TDS proxy flags dissolved-load changes source and product water when relevant Temperature affects UV performance, battery behavior, and process notes enclosure and water stream

docs.title: Control Goals
docs.text:
The first control layer should be boring and robust:

  • prevent dry running
  • shut down on dangerous overpressure
  • log runtime and fault states
  • expose a manual override for service work
  • leave room for future telemetry without depending on it

docs.title: Logging
docs.text:
At minimum, log:

  • timestamps
  • pump runtime
  • fault events
  • filter differential pressure
  • measured or estimated treated volume

docs.title: Alarm Philosophy
docs.text: Good alarms are actionable. Avoid vague indicators like "service soon" unless they map to a clear threshold such as pressure rise, runtime hours, or UV lamp age.
